title: Larva Tending
desc: The nursery needs hands. The larvae need warmth. Don't get attached.

steps.text:
The nursery chambers are deep inside Mount Pillar — warm, humid, lit by the soft glow of cultivated fungus. The air smells like damp earth and something faintly sweet. Rows of nesting pods line the walls, each containing a Danian larva in various stages of development.

The nursery keeper is gentle but brisk. "Temperature shift overnight. The larvae in pod-bank six need to be moved to the warmer chambers near the reservoir heat vents. They're at a critical stage — too cold and development stalls. Too warm and it accelerates unevenly."

She hands you a transport cradle — a padded frame designed to carry three pods at a time. "Smooth movements. No jolts. They're sensitive to vibration at this stage. Talk to them if you want — they can't hear yet, but the vibration is soothing."

options.label: Transport the pods — steady and careful
Check the heat vents first — make sure they're ready

steps.text:
You load three pods onto the cradle. They're surprisingly light — the larvae inside are tiny, curled, translucent enough that you can see a faint pulse through the pod wall. Alive. Growing. Depending on you to not drop them.

The walk to the reservoir chambers is four hundred feet through winding corridors. You move at half-speed, arms steady, watching every step. The cradle's padding absorbs minor vibrations, but a stumble would be catastrophic.

You talk to them, feeling slightly foolish. "Almost there. Warmer chambers ahead. You'll like it — good temperature, consistent humidity, the fungal glow is nicer down here."

The larvae don't respond. Obviously. But the pods seem to pulse slightly warmer in your hands. The keeper said vibration is soothing — maybe your voice counts.

Four trips. Twelve pods relocated. The warmer chamber is perfect — the reservoir's geothermal vents keep the air at a steady temperature, and the humidity is naturally higher. You settle the last pod into its rack.

The keeper checks each pod. All twelve larvae are stable, pulses strong, development on track. "Good transport. Not everyone has steady hands." She marks the transfer in the nursery log. "These will emerge in about three weeks. If you want, you can come back for the emergence. It's... something to see."

options.label: Promise to come back for the emergence

steps.text:
The heat vents are natural geothermal outlets — fissures in the rock that release warm air from deep underground. The nursery uses them as climate control, positioning pod-banks to receive consistent warmth without direct heat.

You check the vents in the destination chamber. Three are active — warm air flowing steadily. The fourth is barely producing. You hold your hand over the outlet. Lukewarm. Not enough.

The vent maintenance hatch reveals the problem — mineral buildup in the fissure throat, narrowing the airflow. You chip it away with a small pick, carefully, breaking the calcified deposits without damaging the rock.

The vent opens up. Warm air rushes out — not hot, but steady and consistent. You check the temperature with a calibration strip. Optimal range.

With all four vents active, the chamber temperature stabilizes perfectly. You arrange the pod-racks to receive even airflow from each vent — not too close (direct heat causes uneven development), not too far (cold spots).

The keeper arrives with the first transport and nods at your vent arrangement. "You cleared the blocked vent. Good — I've been requesting maintenance on that for two weeks." She places the first pods in the racks. "The larvae respond to consistent temperature more than anything else. Consistency is the hive's gift to the next generation."

Twelve pods are relocated to a chamber that's ready for them because you prepared it first.

options.label: Watch the pods settle into their new warmth
